input: Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to drive a 6-digit 7-segment LED display, showing a countdown, displaying hexadecimal messages, and finally displaying the current time (HHMMSS).
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (any model) to blink 8 LEDs connected to the first 8 GPIO pins (wiringPi pin numbering) in a sequential manner, turning them on and off with a 100ms delay.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read temperature data from an Omron D6T-32L-01A thermal sensor connected via I2C, and print the sensor readings (PTAT and pixel temperatures) to the console. The sensor should be connected to the default I2C bus (/dev/i2c-1) using the address 0x0A.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read temperature data from an Omron D6T-44L-06 thermal sensor connected via I2C and print the temperature of each pixel and the sensor's internal temperature to the console.

Hardware Requirements: Raspberry Pi, Omron D6T-44L-06 thermal sensor, I2C connection (SDA, SCL, VCC, GND). The I2C lines should be connected to the appropriate GPIO pins on the Raspberry Pi (typically SDA1 - GPIO2, SCL1 - GPIO3).

Software Dependencies:  WiringPi or similar I2C library (though this code uses direct /dev/i2c access and doesn't explicitly depend on WiringPi), standard C libraries.

Code Explanation: The code initializes the I2C interface, reads temperature data from the D6T-44L-06 sensor, performs PEC (Packet Error Check) validation, converts the raw data to Celsius, and prints the sensor's internal temperature (PTAT) and the temperature of each of the 16 pixels (4x4 array) to the console. The `i2c_read_reg8` function handles I2C communication. The `D6T_checkPEC` function verifies data integrity.  The `conv8us_s16_le` function converts the received bytes into signed 16-bit integers in little-endian format.

Expected Outputs and Behavior: The program continuously reads and prints temperature data to the console. Each line of output includes the PTAT temperature and the temperature of each of the 16 pixels in degrees Celsius. The program runs indefinitely, with a delay between each reading. If the PEC check fails, an error message will be printed to stderr.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read temperature data from an Omron D6T-8L thermal sensor connected via I2C and print the ambient temperature (PTAT) and 8x8 pixel temperatures to the console.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read temperature data from an Omron D6T-8LH thermal sensor connected via I2C and print the ambient temperature (PTAT) and the temperature of each of the 8x8 pixels to the console.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to decode infrared (IR) remote signals received via an IR receiver connected to GPIO24 (WiringPi pin 5), and control an LED connected to GPIO25 (WiringPi pin 6) based on specific key presses.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to control a robot car with an IR remote, controlling motor direction/speed, RGB LED color, a relay, and a servo motor, using an IR receiver connected to GPIO 5.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to control a DC motor using an L293D motor driver, connected to GPIO pins 17, 27, and 22, and make the motor rotate forward, stop, reverse, and stop in a loop.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (any model) to blink an LED connected to GPIO18 (WiringPi pin 1) with a 500ms on/off cycle, printing status messages to the console. It requires the WiringPi library.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (any model) to create a chasing LED effect using 8 LEDs connected to GPIO pins 18, 23, 24, 25, 12, 16, 20, and 21, requiring the wiringPi library, where the LEDs light up sequentially and then turn off sequentially in a loop.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to control an 8x8 LED matrix, sequentially lighting up each row and then turning them off in a loop, using the wiringPi library. The LED matrix rows are connected to GPIO pins defined in the 'y' array and columns to the 'x' array.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to blink an LED connected to a PiFace interface board, using the wiringPi library to control the LED's on/off state with a 500ms delay.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read temperature data from an LM35 temperature sensor connected to a PCF8591 ADC (Channel A0), display the temperature on an LCD, and print it to the console. The LCD is connected to wiringPi pins defined in the `lcdInit` function. Requires wiringPi and pcf8591 libraries. The code reads the analog value from the PCF8591, converts it to a temperature in Celsius, reverses the digits of the temperature value, displays it on the LCD, and prints it to the console every second.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (any model) to detect motion using a PIR sensor connected to GPIO18 (wiringPi pin 1) and control an LED connected to GPIO5 (wiringPi pin 21), printing "somebody" or "nobody" to the console based on motion detection.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read analog data from PCF8591 ADC channel A0, output the value to the console, and then output the same value to the PCF8591 DAC. The PCF8591 is connected via I2C.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (any model) to play a musical scale (Do, Re, Mi, Fa, So, La, Si, Do) using a passive buzzer connected to GPIO18, with each note lasting 300ms, and a 2-second pause between repetitions. The program requires the `wiringPi` library.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to play a pre-defined song using a passive buzzer connected to GPIO18, repeatedly playing the melody. The program utilizes the wiringPi library for PWM control.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read analog data from a photoresistor connected to a PCF8591 ADC (channel A0) and control an LED connected to GPIO 21, turning it on when the light level is above a threshold.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (any model) to control an RGB LED connected to GPIO pins 5 (Red), 4 (Green), and 1 (Blue) using PWM, creating random color changes every 100ms.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (any model) to control three LEDs (red, yellow, blue) based on input from three buttons (red, yellow, blue) and a reset button. The program will illuminate the corresponding LED when its button is pressed until the reset button is pressed.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to blink an LED connected to GPIO1 (WiringPi pin 1) to signal the SOS distress signal (Morse code: ...---...) repeatedly, with console output indicating the dots and dashes.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to control a servo motor connected to GPIO18 (WiringPi pin 1), sweeping it between two fixed positions repeatedly with a 1-second pause at each extreme.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to blink an LED connected to GPIO pin 1 (WiringPi pin numbering) with a 500ms on/off cycle, utilizing the WiringPi library. The program should continuously blink the LED indefinitely.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to control an LED based on sound detected by a sound sensor connected to a PCF8591 ADC. The program reads the analog value from the sound sensor and toggles the LED on/off when the sound level exceeds a threshold.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to simulate a traffic light sequence using LEDs connected to GPIO pins 18 (Red), 24 (Green), and 23 (Yellow). The program requires the wiringPi library and cycles through red, blinking green, and yellow lights indefinitely.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to control a 28BYJ-48 stepper motor connected to GPIO pins 22, 27, 17, and 4, causing it to rotate counterclockwise then clockwise, with pauses in between.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to measure distance using an ultrasonic sensor (HC-SR04) connected to GPIO pins 4 (Trig) and 5 (Echo), and display the measured distance in centimeters on the console.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to decode infrared (IR) remote control signals received by an IR receiver connected to GPIO 24 (WiringPi pin 5) and print the decoded key value to the console.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read X and Y coordinates from a joystick connected to a PCF8591 ADC on address 0x48, and read the joystick button connected to GPIO 26, printing the values to the console.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (any model) to control a relay module connected to GPIO18 (WiringPi pin 1), toggling it on for 5 seconds and off for 1 second repeatedly, while printing the relay state to the console.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to monitor soil moisture using a PCF8591 ADC connected to an analog soil moisture sensor and control a relay connected to BCM GPIO 18, switching it on when the moisture level (analog value from A0) falls below 30.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to initialize the bcm2835 library, then close it, ensuring the program runs with root privileges and outputs nothing if successful, or an error message if not.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (any model) to monitor a water sensor connected to a PCF8591 ADC via analog input A0, and activate a buzzer connected to GPIO18 if the sensor value exceeds a threshold of 30. The program continuously prints the sensor value to the console.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to control the brightness of an LED connected to GPIO1, using an analog input from a PCF8591 analog-to-digital converter (ADC) at address 0x48 connected via I2C. The program reads analog values from the PCF8591 and uses PWM to vary the LED brightness.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to control an LED connected to GPIO 27 using a button connected to GPIO 18, toggling the LED on every odd number of button presses.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read temperature and humidity data from a DHT11 sensor connected to GPIO18 (WiringPi pin 1), and print the readings to the console every 3 seconds.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (any model) to detect flame using a flame sensor connected to GPIO18 (wiringPi pin 1) and activate a buzzer connected to GPIO27 (wiringPi pin 2) when a flame is detected, printing the sensor value to the console.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (any model) to print "Hello World!" to the console every second using the wiringPi library. The program requires the wiringPi library to be installed.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read gas sensor data from an MQ-2 sensor connected via a PCF8591 ADC, display the sensor value on an LCD1602 display, and activate a buzzer if the gas level exceeds a threshold.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to display digits 0-9 sequentially on a single 7-segment LED display, with each digit displayed for 1 second. The 7-segment display's segments (a-g) are connected to GPIO pins 4, 1, 22, 23, 24, 5, and 6 respectively.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to display a counter (0000-9999) on a 4-digit 7-segment LED display, incrementing every second. The display segments are connected to specific GPIO pins as defined in the code.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to display a sequence of numbers 0-9 on a 7-segment display using a 74HC595 shift register connected to GPIO 17 (dataPin), GPIO 22 (latchPin), and GPIO 27 (clockPin), with wiringPi library as a dependency, outputting each number for 1 second.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to control an active buzzer using a button. The buzzer, connected to GPIO 27, sounds when the button connected to GPIO 18 is pressed, using the WiringPi library.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (any model) to control the brightness of two LEDs based on the state of a ball tilt sensor, using GPIO pins 17 (LED1), 27 (LED2), and 18 (tiltPin). The program will adjust the PWM duty cycle of the LEDs, increasing one while decreasing the other, depending on the tilt sensor's output.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to monitor a button press and toggle two LEDs. The button is connected to GPIO0, LED1 to GPIO1, and LED2 to GPIO7; the program uses the WiringPi library.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (any model) to control an LED connected to GPIO1 (WiringPi pin 1) to create a breathing effect by gradually increasing and decreasing its brightness using PWM, and print the PWM value to the console.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (any model) to create a breathing LED effect on an LED connected to GPIO1 using software PWM, printing the PWM value to the console.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to blink an LED connected to GPIO 16 at a rate of 1 second on and 1 second off, exiting gracefully when Ctrl+C is pressed, using the pigpio library.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to blink an LED connected to GPIO 16 at a rate of 1 second on and 1 second off, exiting gracefully upon receiving a Ctrl+C signal.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to simulate a traffic light sequence using LEDs connected to GPIO pins 16 (Red), 19 (Yellow), and 26 (Green), utilizing the pigpio library.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to create a chasing light effect using 6 LEDs connected to GPIO pins 16, 19, 26, 5, 6, and 25, which cycles through turning each LED on for 3 seconds and off for 1 second until Ctrl+C is pressed.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to control a Red LED connected to GPIO16 using a button connected to GPIO6, utilizing the pigpio library and handling Ctrl+C exit.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to control three LEDs using three buttons, where each button toggles the state of its corresponding LED. The program should use the pigpio library for GPIO control and handle interrupts for a clean exit.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to control a buzzer connected to GPIO16, toggling it on and off every 2 seconds until Ctrl+C is pressed, then cleaning up the GPIO.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to display numbers 0-9 sequentially on a 7-segment LED display, with each number displayed for 1 second, using the pigpio library and handling Ctrl+C to exit.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read button presses from a PiFace interface board and toggle corresponding LEDs, providing console output indicating button state changes.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to control an 8-LED multiplexed display connected to GPIO pins 16 (DATA), 5 (LATCH), and 6 (CLOCK), displaying a sequence of patterns until a CTRL-C signal is received.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to control an 8x8 LED matrix, creating a simple animation by sequentially lighting up rows and columns, and gracefully exiting on CTRL-C.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (any model) to blink an LED connected to GPIO0 (WiringPi pin 0) with a 500ms on/off cycle. The program requires the WiringPi library and will print "LED on..." and "LED off..." to the console during operation.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to control an RGB LED connected to GPIO pins 0, 1, and 2, cycling through red, green, blue, yellow, purple, and cyan colors with a 500ms delay between each color change, while printing the current color to the console.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to create an LED bar graph animation using 10 LEDs connected to GPIO pins 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9, and 10, cycling through odd, even, and all LEDs lighting up sequentially.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to display hexadecimal digits 0-F sequentially on a 7-segment display controlled by an HC595 shift register connected to GPIO pins 0, 1, and 2.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to display a 4-digit number that increments every second on a 4-digit 7-segment display using a 74HC595 shift register, with SDI connected to GPIO5, RCLK to GPIO4, SRCLK to GPIO1, and digit selection pins to GPIO12, GPIO3, GPIO2, and GPIO0.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to control an LED matrix using two cascaded 74HC595 shift registers, displaying a predefined animation pattern based on the `code_H` and `code_L` arrays.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to display "Greetings!" on the first line and "From SunFounder" on the second line of an I2C LCD1602 display.

Hardware Requirements: Raspberry Pi, LCD1602 I2C display module (typically with a PCF8574 I2C interface). Connect the LCD1602 module to the Raspberry Pi's I2C pins (SDA, SCL), VCC (3.3V or 5V), and GND.

Software Dependencies: wiringPi library (install with `sudo apt-get install wiringpi`).

Explanation: The code initializes the LCD1602 display via I2C using the wiringPi library. It defines functions to send commands and data to the LCD, handling the necessary bit manipulation for the I2C communication protocol. The `main` function initializes the I2C connection, clears the display, and writes the specified strings to the first and second lines of the LCD.

Expected Outputs and Behavior: The LCD1602 display will show "Greetings!" on the first line and "From SunFounder" on the second line. The backlight of the LCD should be on (if `BLEN` is set to 1).

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to control an active buzzer connected to GPIO0, creating a simple beeping sound with 100ms on and 100ms off intervals. The program requires the WiringPi library and will print "Buzzer on" and "Buzzer off" messages to the console accordingly.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to display an analog clock with date and time on a 128x64 parallel interface LCD, connected to GPIOs 0-7 (data), 10 (CS1), 11 (CS2), 12 (STROBE), and 10 (RS), utilizing the WiringPi library.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to control a DC motor, connected to GPIO pins 0, 2, and 3, to rotate clockwise, stop, rotate counter-clockwise, and stop, repeating indefinitely. The program utilizes the WiringPi library.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to control a servo motor connected to GPIO1, making it sweep back and forth between 0 and 180 degrees continuously. The program requires the WiringPi library and softPwm library for PWM control.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to control a stepper motor connected to GPIO pins 1, 4, 5, and 6, allowing the user to select clockwise or anti-clockwise rotation via the terminal. The program requires the WiringPi library and will continuously rotate the motor in the selected direction.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to simulate and control RPM using a button connected to GPIO17, incrementing it in steps of 100 between 4000 and 8000, and printing the current RPM to the console.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 4 to control two brushless DC motors (BLDC) using ESCs, rotary encoders, and PID/Sliding Mode Control, maintaining target angles for both main and tail rotors.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 3B+ to read input from the CrowPi's built-in 4x4 button matrix and print the corresponding key number to the console when a button is pressed, utilizing the WiringPi library.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 3B+ to control a buzzer using a button, where pressing the button activates the buzzer and releasing it deactivates it, utilizing WiringPi library.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 3B+ to measure distance using an ultrasonic sensor connected to GPIO36 (TRIG) and GPIO32 (ECHO), and print the distance in centimeters and inches to the console every second.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi Pico to display serial input on an SSD1306 OLED display and an LED matrix, toggle LEDs via button presses, and print status messages to the serial console.

Hardware Requirements: Raspberry Pi Pico, SSD1306 OLED display (I2C), 8x8 LED Matrix, two push buttons, two LEDs (Green and Blue), resistors. Connect SSD1306 to I2C pins (SDA, SCL), LED Matrix to a GPIO pin (defined as MATRIX_GPIO_PIN in libs/matrix.h), buttons to GPIO 5 and 6, and LEDs to GPIO 11 and 12 with appropriate current limiting resistors.

Software Dependencies: Requires the Raspberry Pi Pico SDK, including libraries for I2C, GPIO, PIO, and custom libraries for the LED matrix (`libs/matrix.h`) and SSD1306 display (`libs/ssd1306.h`, `libs/font.h`).

The program initializes the SSD1306 display and LED matrix. It continuously reads serial input, displays the input string on the OLED display, and displays the corresponding number on the LED matrix if the input is a single digit. Buttons A and B toggle the Green and Blue LEDs respectively, displaying status messages on the OLED and serial console. Debouncing is implemented for the button presses. The LED matrix display is handled using the PIO.

Expected outputs: The serial input is displayed on the SSD1306 display. If the serial input is a single digit (0-9), the corresponding number is displayed on the LED matrix. Pressing button A toggles the Green LED and displays a message on the OLED and serial console. Pressing button B toggles the Blue LED and displays a message on the OLED and serial console. The program continuously loops, reading serial input and responding to button presses.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (any model) to test the accuracy of the `usleep()` function for delays between 1 and 199 microseconds, reporting minimum, maximum, under-run, over-run, exact matches, average delay, and descheduling counts. The program uses `gettimeofday()` to measure the actual delay and prints the results to the console.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read temperature data from an Arduino connected via I2C at address 0x04, and output the temperature along with the current timestamp as an HTML page.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to control a cooling fan based on temperature readings from a sensor connected to GPIO0, with the fan connected to GPIO1, using the WiringPi library. The program simulates temperature readings and turns the fan on if the temperature exceeds 30 degrees Celsius.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 3B+ to detect motion using a motion sensor connected to GPIO16 (physical pin 16) and print "Motion detected!" to the console when motion is sensed.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to interface with a DS1302 Real-Time Clock (RTC) module, allowing the user to read the current date and time, set the system clock from the RTC, set the RTC from the system clock, and perform a RAM test on the DS1302.

Hardware Requirements: DS1302 RTC module, connecting pins (RST, DAT, CLK) to GPIO pins 0, 1, and 2 respectively.
Software Dependencies: WiringPi library.
Code Explanation: The code uses the WiringPi library to communicate with the DS1302 RTC module. It provides functions to read and write the RTC clock registers and RAM. The main function allows the user to choose between reading the clock, setting the Linux clock from the RTC, setting the RTC from the Linux clock, or running a RAM test.
Expected Outputs and Behavior: When run without arguments, the program continuously prints the current date and time read from the DS1302. With the "-slc" argument, it sets the Linux system clock to the time read from the DS1302. With the "-sdsc" argument, it sets the DS1302 clock to the current Linux system time. With the "-rtest" argument, it performs a RAM test on the DS1302 and prints the results.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read data from an MPU6050 sensor connected via I2C and continuously print the accelerometer, gyroscope, and temperature readings to the console.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to interact with a custom sensor driver named 'sa_driver_dev' through character device file, allowing users to activate/deactivate the sensor and exit the application using console-based commands.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to interact with a sensor driver named 'sa_driver_dev' via character device, allowing the user to activate, deactivate, or exit the sensor application through console input. The program requires the 'sa_app.h' header file and the 'sa_driver_dev' kernel module to be loaded, and it communicates with the driver using the open, read, and close system calls.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 3B+ to detect sound using a sound sensor connected to GPIO18 (physical pin 12) and toggle an LED connected to GPIO22 (physical pin 15) upon sound detection. The program should print "Sound detected" to the console when sound is detected.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to generate a sine wave on the Gertboard D/A port 0, read the corresponding value from the A/D port 0, and display it as a scrolling oscilloscope on the terminal.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to record 1 second of audio using `arecord`, display the WAV header and waveform on a connected screen, and send the audio samples, repeating this process 10 times.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 3B+ to control a buzzer and vibration module using two buttons, with the buzzer activated by pressing BUTTON_UP and the vibration module activated by pressing BUTTON_DN.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (tested on 3 B+ but should work on other models) to read temperature and humidity data from the HTS221 sensor on the Sense HAT add-on board via I2C and print the calibrated readings to the console.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (tested on 3 B+) to control the Sense HAT LED matrix, displaying a red color across the entire matrix, flashing white three times, and then clearing the display.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 3 B+ to read temperature and pressure data from the LPS25H sensor on a Sense HAT via I2C and print the readings to the console.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to output a binary counter on a Quick2Wire 16-bit GPIO expansion board (MCP23017) connected via I2C, using pins 100-109 as outputs and monitoring input pins 0 (onboard) and 115 (expansion board).

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to create an interrupt-driven oscillator by connecting GPIO0 (output) to GPIO1 (input), triggering an interrupt on a falling edge on GPIO1, and toggling GPIO0 within the interrupt service routine, reporting the interrupt frequency.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to monitor GPIO pins 0-7 for falling edge interrupts and print the interrupt count for each pin to the console. The program uses the WiringPi library and expects external signals to trigger the interrupts.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (any model) to test interrupt handling using WiringPi. The program configures a GPIO pin as an interrupt and triggers it using another GPIO pin, measuring the interrupt response time for rising, falling, and both edges.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to implement a "Pi Ladder" game using a PiFace interface, where the user charges a virtual capacitor by holding a button, and the goal is to light up all LEDs in a bargraph display.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to drive an Adafruit RGB LCD Plate connected via MCP23017 I2C port expander, displaying a scrolling message, the current time, and allowing the user to change the backlight color using the up/down buttons.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to drive an HD44780-compatible LCD connected via parallel interface (either 8-bit or 4-bit mode) to display a scrolling message, the current time, and a custom character, using the wiringPi library.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to monitor the Raspberry Pi's low-power signal on GPIO 35, triggering an interrupt when the signal falls and logging the event with a timestamp to the console.

Hardware requirements: Raspberry Pi, connection to the low-power signal (GPIO 35). Software dependencies: wiringPi library. The code configures GPIO 35 as an interrupt pin, triggering the `lowPower` function when the signal transitions from high to low (falling edge). The `lowPower` function gets the current time and prints a message to the console indicating that a low power event has been detected. The main loop then delays for 1 second and repeats indefinitely. The expected output is a continuous stream of "LOW POWER DETECTED" messages with timestamps printed to the console whenever the low-power signal goes low.
output:
/*
 * Original Filename: lowPower.c
 * Source Tracking: source_file_26
 * Generated by: GitHub Scraping
 */

/*
 * lowPower.c:
 *	Check the Pi's LOW-Power signal.
 *
 *	This is a demonstration program that could be turned into some sort
 *	of logger via e.g. syslog - however it's also probably something
 *	that might be better handled by a future kernel - who knows.
 *
 * Copyright (c) 2014 Gordon Henderson.
 ***********************************************************************
 * This file is part of wiringPi:
 *	https://github.com/WiringPi/WiringPi/
 *
 *    wiringPi is free software: you can redistribute it and/or modify
 *    it under the terms of the GNU Lesser General Public License as published by
 *    the Free Software Foundation, either version 3 of the License, or
 *    (at your option) any later version.
 *
 *    wiringPi is distributed in the hope that it will be useful,
 *    but WITHOUT ANY WARRANTY; without even the implied warranty of
 *    MERCHANTABILITY or FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE.  See the
 *    GNU Lesser General Public License for more details.
 *
 *    You should have received a copy of the GNU Lesser General Public License
 *    along with wiringPi.  If not, see <http://www.gnu.org/licenses/>.
 ***********************************************************************
 */

#include <stdio.h>
#include <time.h>
#include <wiringPi.h>


#define	LOW_POWER	35

/*
 * lowPower:
 *	This is an ISR that waits for the low-power signal going low and
 *	prints the result.
 *********************************************************************************
 */

void lowPower (void)
{
  time_t t ;

  time (&t) ;
  printf ("%s: LOW POWER DETECTED\n", ctime (&t)) ;
}


/*
 *********************************************************************************
 * main
 *********************************************************************************
 */

int main (void)
{
  wiringPiSetupGpio () ;	// GPIO mode as it's an internal pin

  wiringPiISR (LOW_POWER, INT_EDGE_FALLING, &lowPower) ;

  for (;;)
    delay (1000) ;

  return 0 ;
}

input: Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read temperature data from two MAX31855 thermocouple amplifier chips connected via SPI, and display the raw readings, error codes, temperature in Celsius, and temperature in Fahrenheit for each sensor on the console.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to blink an LED connected to the on-board GPIO (pin 1) and another LED connected to pin 0 of an MCP23017 I/O expander connected via I2C (address 0x20) using the WiringPi library.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to make the OK/Act LED pulsate by controlling its brightness using the `wiringPi` library and `softPwm` functionality. The program disables the default LED trigger and uses GPIO 16 to control the LED.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to implement a reaction timer game using the PiFace interface board, where the player presses a button corresponding to a lit LED as quickly as possible.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to record audio using the Gertboard analog input connected to pin 100, store it in a buffer, and then play it back through the analog output, measuring the time taken for both operations.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read temperature and humidity data from a RHT03/MaxDetect series sensor connected to GPIO pin 7 using the wiringPi library, and continuously display the current, minimum, and maximum readings.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read accelerometer, gyroscope, and temperature data from an MPU6050 sensor connected via I2C, and print the readings to the console continuously.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to blink an LED connected to GPIO 17 (wiringPi pin 0) and simultaneously print "Hello, world" to the console in an infinite loop.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (any model) to control a Pimoroni Scroll pHAT, allowing users to clear the display, set brightness, plot points/lines, set scroll speed, and scroll text via command-line arguments.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read data from the serial port `/dev/ttyAMA0` at a baud rate of 115200 and print the received characters to the standard output.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to test the serial communication on `/dev/ttyAMA0` at 115200 baud by sending characters from 0-255 and verifying the loopback. The program requires wiringPi library and expects a serial loopback connection.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to control a servo motor connected to GPIO0 using the `softServo` library and wiringPi. The program initializes the softServo library, sets the servo position to 0, and then enters an infinite loop.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to control 8 LEDs using software PWM via the wiringPi library, creating a simple light animation sequence on GPIO pins 0-7.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to play a musical scale using a piezo buzzer connected to GPIO pin 3, utilizing the `wiringPi` and `softTone` libraries to generate tones.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to measure and compare the speed of different GPIO access methods using the WiringPi library, outputting the time taken and iterations per second for each method.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to measure and report SPI communication speed and latency for different speeds and data sizes using the wiringPi library. The program utilizes SPI channel 0 and requires the wiringPi library to be installed.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read temperature data from two LM35 temperature sensors connected to the Gertboard's analog input channels 0 and 1, and display the temperature readings in Celsius on the console.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to test the Pimoroni Scroll pHAT, demonstrating its basic functionalities like pixel lighting, shape drawing, intensity control, and scrolling text display. The program requires the Scroll pHAT hardware connected to the Raspberry Pi and the `wiringPi` and `scrollPhat` libraries, and it will display various patterns and a scrolling message on the pHAT's LED matrix, prompting the user to observe the changes.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to blink an LED connected to GPIO 17 (wiringPi pin 0) with a 500ms on/off cycle, using the wiringPi library.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to implement a simple voltmeter using the Gertboard A/D converter, reading analog voltages from two channels and displaying them on the console. The Gertboard must be connected according to its documentation, and wiringPi must be installed.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read analog voltages from a Quick2Wire analog board (Q2W) connected via I2C using the PCF8591 ADC and print the voltage readings to the console, while also toggling the on-board LED.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to implement a VU meter using a microphone connected to the Gertboard analog input 0, displaying the audio level as a percentage on 8 LEDs connected to the Gertboard digital outputs 0-7.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (any model) to detect button presses on GPIO17 using interrupts, increment a counter for each press (debounced), and print the counter value to the console every time it changes.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to control a fan based on temperature readings. The program uses a temperature sensor connected to GPIO0 and controls a fan connected to GPIO1, turning it on if the temperature exceeds 30 degrees Celsius.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read data from an MPU6050 IMU sensor connected via I2C and print the temperature, accelerometer, and gyroscope readings to the console. The MPU6050 is connected to I2C1 at address 0x68.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read analog values from a PCF8591 ADC connected via I2C and display them on the terminal using ncurses, allowing the user to adjust an analog output value using '+' and '-' keys.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to monitor I2C communication on specified GPIO pins (SCL and SDA) by reading pigpio notifications from a pipe and print the interpreted I2C start, stop, and data bytes to the console.

Hardware Requirements: Raspberry Pi, I2C bus to monitor, pigpio daemon running. The SCL and SDA lines of the I2C bus must be connected to GPIO pins on the Raspberry Pi.

Software Dependencies: pigpio library.

Explanation: The code reads notifications from a pigpio pipe, which is configured to monitor changes on the specified SCL and SDA GPIO pins. It parses the changes in the signal levels to detect I2C start and stop conditions and to extract the data bytes being transmitted. The `parse_I2C` function interprets the SCL and SDA signals and prints the data bytes enclosed within square brackets, where "+" indicates ACK and "-" indicates NACK.

Expected Outputs and Behavior: The program outputs the interpreted I2C traffic to the console, including start and stop conditions and data bytes. It expects the pigpio daemon to be running and notifications to be enabled for the specified SCL and SDA pins. The program will print I2C data in hexadecimal format, enclosed in square brackets, to standard output. The program exits if it cannot read valid data from the pigpio notification pipe.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to measure capacitor charge time using a potentiometer connected to GPIO 25, and print the raw and smoothed readings to the console.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to control 12 LEDs connected to GPIO pins 0-7, 10-13, creating a simple lighting sequence, using the WiringPi library. The program will continuously loop through the sequence.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to generate a precise pulse per second (PPS) signal on a specified GPIO pin, using pigpio library, and print timing statistics to the console. The GPIO pin, pulse length, level, and interval are configurable via command-line arguments.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to control GPIO pins, setting up a servo on GPIO4, PWM on GPIO17, blinking an LED on GPIO18, and mirroring the state of GPIO23 to GPIO24 for 60 seconds using the pigpio library.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read analog data from five MCP3008 ADCs connected to specific GPIO pins using SPI bit-banging with DMA, and print the ADC values to the console.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read data from multiple MCP3202 ADC converters using bit-banged SPI via DMA, and print the converted values to the console.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to simultaneously read analog data from five MCP3XXX ADCs (two MCP3202 12-bit and three MCP3008 10-bit) using bit-banged SPI via DMA, and print the converted digital values to the console. The ADCs are connected to GPIOs 5 (CLK), 12 (MOSI), 25 (SS), 6 (MISO1), 26 (MISO2), 13 (MISO3), 23 (MISO4), and 24 (MISO5); the program depends on the pigpio library and outputs ADC readings continuously until 2,000,000 samples are collected, then prints the sampling rate.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to control servo motors connected to specified GPIO pins, causing them to sweep back and forth within a defined pulse width range, and stop when Ctrl+C is pressed.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read data from a rotary encoder connected to GPIO 7 and 8, and print the encoder position to the console. The program should run for 300 seconds.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read analog values from a PCF8591 ADC/DAC chip connected via I2C and display them in a curses-based user interface, allowing the user to control the DAC output with '+' and '-' keys.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to initialize the GPIO pins, detect the Raspberry Pi model and revision, and then print the mode and level of GPIO pins 0 to 53 to the console.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (any model) to create a 12-LED sequencer connected to the first 4 native Raspberry Pi GPIO pins and 8 pins on the ATmega of a Gertboard, using DRC serial communication, and blink them in a pre-defined sequence indefinitely.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read acceleration data from an ADXL345 accelerometer connected via I2C and print the X, Y, and Z axis acceleration values to the console, using I2C bus 0.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to monitor the frequency of signals on specified GPIO pins and print the frequency readings to the console, allowing for adjustable sampling and reporting rates.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to monitor the frequency of signals on specified GPIO pins (e.g., 4, 7, 8) and report the counts per second to the console, with configurable sample rate and refresh period.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to monitor a Hall effect sensor (OH3144E or equivalent) connected to GPIO 14 (TXD), printing the sensor's state transitions (high/low) and the time elapsed between changes to the console for a specified duration.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (all models) to set the frequency of a general-purpose clock (GPCLK0 or GPCLK2) and output it on GPIO4 (GPCLK0) or GPIO6 (GPCLK2), allowing frequency or divider-based configuration.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (all models) to initialize the GPIO library, then iterate through GPIOs 0-53, printing their current tick, mode, and level. Finally, it prints the first 16 registers of the BSC peripheral.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (any model) to generate a specified number of PWM pulses on GPIO18 (ALT5 function, PWM1), with configurable pulse width and gap between pulses, using the PWM hardware.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 5 to read temperature data from a DS18B20 sensor connected to a specified GPIO pin, optionally scan for sensors, change resolution, or read sensor IDs from a file and output temperature readings.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to control 6 LEDs connected to a Gertduino board via DRC serial communication, creating a blinking sequence with adjustable speed using two input buttons.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read temperature data from multiple DS18B20 temperature sensors connected to various GPIO pins specified as command-line arguments, using the gpiod library for parallel reading, and print the temperature readings with their resolutions.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (all models) to read temperature data from DS18B20 sensor(s) connected via GPIO, optionally setting resolution, scanning for sensor IDs, or reading IDs from a file, and printing the temperature readings.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (any model) to read temperature data from multiple DS18B20 temperature sensors connected to various GPIO pins, using a parallel reading method, and print the temperature readings to the console.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 4 to read temperature data from a DS18B20 sensor connected via bit-banging on a specified GPIO pin (default GPIO4), and optionally configure the sensor's resolution.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 5 to read temperature data from a DS18B20 sensor connected via bit-banging to a specified GPIO pin, and optionally configure the sensor's resolution, utilizing the gpiod library.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 2 to read temperature and humidity data from a Honeywell HIH8000 series sensor connected via I2C, and print the readings to the console.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to blink an LED connected to GPIO pin 17 (RPi Plug P1 pin 11) with a 0.5 second on/off cycle, utilizing the bcm2835 library.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to detect a falling edge event on GPIO15 and print a message to the console whenever the event occurs. The program uses the bcm2835 library and requires a pull-up resistor to be enabled on the GPIO pin.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read the digital value from GPIO15 (physical pin 15) and print it to the console every 500ms. The pin has a pull-up resistor enabled.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (any model) to control the brightness of an LED connected to GPIO18 (pin 12) using PWM, creating a fading effect.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to blink 8 LEDs connected to GPIO pins starting from a specified base pin, utilizing the `drcNet` library for remote GPIO control over a network. The program will continuously cycle through the LEDs, turning them on and off sequentially.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to demonstrate SPI communication by sending a byte (0x23) and reading back the response, verifying the loopback connection between MOSI and MISO pins. The program requires the `bcm2835` library and root privileges to access SPI.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi (any model) to demonstrate SPI communication by sending a predefined byte array and reading the response, printing the received data to the console.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read flow rate data from an Omron D6F-PH flow sensor connected via I2C and print the flow rate in liters per minute to the console.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read flow rate data from an OMRON D6F-PH flow sensor connected via I2C and print the flow rate in liters per minute to the console.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read flow rate data from an Omron D6F-PH flow sensor connected via I2C and print the flow rate in liters per minute to the console.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read flow rate data from an Omron D6F-PH flow sensor connected via I2C and print the flow rate in liters per minute to the console.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read differential pressure data from an Omron D6F-PH0025 differential pressure sensor connected via I2C and print the pressure reading in Pascals to the console continuously.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi 5 to read differential pressure data from an Omron D6F-PH0505 differential pressure sensor connected via I2C and print the pressure readings in Pascals to the console.

Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read differential pressure data from an Omron D6F-PH5050 differential pressure sensor connected via I2C and print the pressure readings in Pascals to the console.
Write a C program for Raspberry Pi to read temperature data from an Omron D6T-1A thermal sensor connected via I2C and print the ambient temperature (PTAT) and pixel temperature to the console.
